California
Fair POlitical
Practices Commission
April 11, 1991
Bradley w. Hertz
REED & DAVIDSON
550 South Fl-ower street, 7th Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90071
Re: Your Request for Inforrnal Assistance
Our File No. I - 9 O-rGr-
bb3
Dear Mr. Hertz:
You have requested advice regarding the conflict of interest
prcvisions of the Political 
Reform Act athe rrasgrrl.l since your
Let:er does not involve a specific governmental decis-ion, we are
treating your request as onl for iniorinal assistance.2
OUESTI ON
Is a purchaser of advertising space from a newspaper a source
of income to the owner of the newspaper. and, if so, would the
newspaper be considered a retail business subject to the exceptj-on
for income fron retail sales found in Section 87103.5?
CONCLUS I ON
A purchaser of advertising space from a ner,rspaper is a source
of income to the owner of the newspaper. Ho$/ever, the exception
found in Section 87103.5 for retaj.l. sales applies to the sa.Le of
ner./spaper advertising space. Therefore, the olrner of the
1 cor".n."nt code sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Governnent Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
18Ooo, et seo. AIl references to regulations are to Title 2,
D j-vision 5 of the CaLifornia code of Regulations.
2 Inforr'ral assistance does not provide the requestor wj.th the
j.mmunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(covernment Code Section 83114; 2 Cal-. Code of Regs.
Section 18329 (c) (3). )

nerspaper would not have to disqua),ify hirnself frorn decisions
affecting an advertiser if the retail customers of the ne!/spaper
constitute a signif j,cant segment of the public generally, and the
amount of incorne received by the nehrspaper frorn the advertiser is
not distinguishable from the anount of incone received frorn its
other retail custorners.
FACTS
Your client is a city councilnember who owns and controls a
weekly newspaper. A corporation which advertises in the nehrspaper
may come before the city counciL on a matter which will have a
materiat financial effect on that corporation. The advertising
purchased in the t',re Ive nonths preceding the council decision
amounts to more than S25o.
You are questioning whether the corporation is a source of
income to the newspaper owner and, if so, whether the exception
found in Section 87103.5 for income from retail sales zould apply.
so that the councilmenber could participate in a decision
maier ia l Iy affecting the corporation.
ANALYSI S
As a cj.ty councilrember, your client is a public cfficial 
of
a Iocal- governlient agency (see Sections 820'i3 and 320':i) and is,
therefore, subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the
section 87100 provides that "tnlo public of f i.ciaL at any
level of state or local government shall nake, participate in
inakl.ng or in any way use his official 
position to influence a
covernnent-al. decision in $rhich he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest. " Section 87103 defines "financial
interest", in pertinent part, as follows:
An official 
has a financial interest in a
decision within the rneaning of Section 87100 if i:
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial. effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on:
***
(c) Any source of income, other than loans by
a comrnercial Iending institution 
in the regular
course of business, aggregatj.ng two hundred fifty
dollars (5250) or more in val.ue received by or
promised to the public official 
within twelve
T":a:= prior to the tine $/hen the decision is made;


Cur FlIe No. I-90--H
Page 3 
bb?
You have not indicated that there are owners of the ne$/spaper
other than the councilmenber you represent. we assume that the
counciLmenber owns 10 percent or more of the newspaper.
Therefore, any source of income to the newspaPer in the twelve
months precedinq a governmental. decision woul.d be considered a
source of j.ncome to the counciLmenber, if his prorata share of the
payments was S25o or more. (Sections 82030 and 87103(c).)
However, as you have correctLy noted in your letter, there
is, in section 87103.5, an exception to section 87103(c) for
income from retail saIes.
Section 87103.5 sets forth the following:
Notr"rithstand ing subdivision (c) of Section
87103, a retail customer of a business entity
engaged in retail sales of goods or services to the
public generally is not a source of income to an
official 
e/ho or.,rns a 1o-percent or greater interest
in the entity if the retail customers of the
business entity constitute a signifi.cant segment of
the public aenerally, and the anount of income
received by the business entity from the custoner
is not distinguishable from the anount cf income
received from i.ts other retail customers.
A newspaper, which seils space to advertisers and provides
distributj.on to the public of the advertisements placed by
ad.rertising customers. is considered a retail business for
purposes of Section 87103.5.
Regulation 18703.5, copy enclosed, clarifies 
Section 87103.5
as folfows:
(a) For purposes of covernment code section
37103.5, the retail customers of a business entity
constitute a significant segrent of the public
generally if either of the following is true:
(1) The retaj.l custoners of the business
entity during the preceding 12 months are
suf f j,cient in nurnber to equal 10 percent or
more of the population or households of the
jurisdiction; 
or
(2) The retail custoners of the business
entity during the preceding 12 months number
at least ten thousand.
For purposes of this subdivision, a customer
of a retail business entity is each separate and
distinct purchaser cf goods or services, whether an

Page 4
I-90-―
ιl多
individual, household, business or other entity.
rf records are not maj.ntained by customer name, a
good faith estinate shaII be made to determine what
percentage of sales transactions represent nultiple
transactions by repeat customers. The total number
of sales transactions shall then be reduced by the
estinated percentage of repeat customers to yield
the number of customers for purposes of applying
this subdivision.
(b) For purposes of covernment Code Section
87103.5, the anount of incone rece j,ved by a
business entity from a retail custoner is not
d j. stingu ishable from the amount of income received
from its cther retail customers if the amount spent
by the customer in question during the preceding 12
months is less than one-tenth of I percent of the
gross sales revenues of the retail business entity
lor the preceding fiscal year.
(c) For purposes of covernnent code Section
87100, an official 
who owns 10 percent or more of a
retaiL business entity vrhich meets the criteria 
i.n
either subdivisj.on (a) (I) or (a) (2), does not "have
reason to knowrr that a deci-sion will affect a
source of income to the retail business enti-.1- ;hen
either of the following applies:
(1) If all of the following are true:
(A) The customer does not have a
charge account or open book account with
the retail 
bus iness,'
(B) The retail business does not
maintain records for noncharge customer
transactions by customer name or other
method for tracking transactions which
would provide the custoner name,' and
(C) The fact that the person is a
custoner is not personally kno\.rn to the
official; 
or
(2) If all of the following are true:
(A) The accounts and books of the
retail business entity are maintained by
someone other than the official 
or a
member of the officiaL,s inrnediate
f ani J,y; and


Our Fi■e No. I-90-`了
」
Page 5         01多
(B) The fact that the person is a
customer is not personaLly known to the
official.
(d) For purposes of subdivision (c), a credit
card transaction utilizing a credit card not issued
by the retail business entity is considered a
It noncharge custoner transaction. rl
(e) Subdivision (c) shaIl not be utiLized i.n
deterrninj-ng whether an official 
rrknows'r of a
financial interest in a decision within the rneaning
of covernment code Section 87100. when such
knowLedge exists, or the fact that a person is a
source of incone is brought to the attention of the
of f icial- prior to the governmental decision, the
provisions of subdj.vision (c) shall have no effect
on the of f j.ci.al's duty to disquali.f y.
Thus, as per Regulation 14703.5(b), if the corpora"j-on that
ad.rertises in the subject newspaper provides less than one-tenth
of one percent of the gross advertising sales revenue of the
newspaper, the inccme fron the corporation would be considered tc
be indj"stinguishable from incone prcvided by o'.her retaii
customers, anC the city ccuncilnember would nct have to di-squalily
himself or herself from par'.icipating in deci.sions which woul'd
have a reasonab),y foreseeable material financial effect on the
corporatj.on. Bear in mind. however, that if the circulation of
the newspaper amounts to fe'"rer persons than are required by
Regulation 18703.5(a) (1) and (2), the statutory exception for
owners of retail businesses would not apply, and the corporation
would be considered a source of income to lhe of f icial, under
87103 (c) .
If the criteria for Regulation 18703.5 are not met, then it
must be determined rrhether a governmental decision would have a
reasonably foreseeable, material. financial effect upon the source
of income to the public official.
whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FppC Cps. 198,
copy enclosed. )


our File No. 
■-90-
Page 6        ●ク3
In addition. the foreseeable effect on the councilmenber's
source of income rnust also be material to require
disqualification. 
The test for rnaterial financial effect differs
dependi,ng on the specific circumstances of each decision. For
example, rrhere a source of incone is directly before the city
council, Regulation 18702.1(a) (copy enclosed) provides that the
any financial effect of the decision on a source of incorne is
deemed rnaterial and disqual if ication is required.3 
(combs Advice
Letter, No. A-89-177, copY enclosed. )
where the source of income is not directJ.y before the city
council, but may be indirectly affected, Regulations 18702.2 and
L8702.6 (copies enclosed) app1y. Regulation f8702.6 applies to
situations where a decision affects an individual who is a source
of income of 5250 or nore. Regulatj-on la702.2 provides different
standards of naterj.ality which apply to sources of income that are
business entities depending upon the size of the entity.
For example, Regulation 18702.2(g) provides materiality
standards for srnaller business entities as follows:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of S10,000 or more; or
(2) The decisj.on witl result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of 52,500 or more,' or
(l) 
The decision wiLl result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of S 10, 000 or more.
Finally, where a source of incone is an individual and not
directly before the c j.ty council, Regulation f8702.6 provides:
The effect of a decision is rnaterial. as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official 
if any of the following applies:
(a) The decision will affect the
individual's incorne, i.nvestments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilj.ties
I A =orl.". of incorae is directly before the city council when the
source initiates the proceeding by f j-1j,ng an application, c1aim,
appeal. or simj-lar reguest, or is a named party in, or the subject
of, the proceeding. A person or business entity is the subject of
a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, perrnit, or other
entitlenent to, or contract with, the subject person or busj.ness
entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b) . )


our FiIe No. I-90-63-
Pase 7 
bb)
(other than real- property) by $:.,000 or nore;
or
(b) The decision will affect the
individual's real property interest in a
nanner that is considered material under
Section 18702.3 or Section 18702.4.1
In conclusion, the city councilmember may not participate in
any decision in which a source of income of $250 or nore in the
preceding 12 Donths is directly involved and it is foreseeable
that there will be a financial effect on the source. fn addition,
the councilmenber may not participate in any decision that will
foreseeably and materially affect a source of income indirectly.
The materiatity of the effect would be determined by examining the
extent of the foreseeabLe financial effect on the source of
i ncome .
The Public Generallv Exception
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financj.al effect of a
decision is :naterial, disgua I if ication is required only if the
effect of the decision is distinguishable fron the effect upon the
public genera Ily.
For the "public generalJ,y" excepti.on to appIy, a decision
must affect the official's 
interests in substantially the sane
manner as it r,rouLd affect a significant segTnent of the public.
(Regulation 18703, copy enclosed. ) The "public" consists of the
entire 'jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In re owen (1975)
2 FPPC ops. 77, copy enclosed. ) This is so because aI1 the
residents of the jurisdiction are constituents of the official.
I hope the above answers your questj-on.
further information please do not hesitate to
322-590L.
ff you reguire
contact ue at (916)

infornation.
